title: Restitution / Unjust Enrichment

statuteRef.label: Restitutionary claims — time limit for actions founded on quasi-contract or mistake

questions.label: Date the enrichment arose
questions.helpText: The date on which the defendant received the unjust enrichment — for example, the date of a mistaken payment, the date a benefit was conferred under a failed contract, or the date an overpayment was made.

questions.label: Was the claimant under a disability at the date of accrual?
questions.helpText: Under the Limitation Act 1980, a person is under a disability if they are a minor (under 18) or lack capacity within the meaning of the Mental Capacity Act 2005.

questions.label: Date disability ceased
questions.helpText: The date the claimant turned 18, or the date capacity was regained.

questions.label: Was there fraud, deliberate concealment, or mistake?
questions.helpText: Under s.32(1)(c) Limitation Act 1980, where the action is for relief from the consequences of a mistake, the limitation period does not begin until the claimant discovered or could with reasonable diligence have discovered the mistake. This is particularly relevant to restitution claims based on mistaken payments.

questions.label: Date the mistake was discovered
questions.helpText: The date the claimant discovered, or could with reasonable diligence have discovered, the mistake, fraud, or concealment.

questions.label: Has the defendant acknowledged the obligation to make restitution?
questions.helpText: Under s.29 Limitation Act 1980, a written acknowledgment signed by the person liable causes the limitation period to run afresh.

questions.label: Date of acknowledgment
questions.helpText: The date of the written, signed acknowledgment.

questions.label: Has the defendant made a part repayment?
questions.helpText: Under s.29 Limitation Act 1980, a part payment causes the limitation period to run afresh from the date of payment.

questions.label: Date of part repayment
questions.helpText: The date the part repayment was made.

notes: Restitutionary claims are classified as quasi-contractual for limitation purposes, attracting the 6-year period by analogy with s.5.
The s.32(1)(c) mistake provision is particularly important for mistaken payments — it postpones the start of limitation until the mistake is discovered.
Following Kleinwort Benson v Lincoln City Council [1999], claims to recover money paid under a mistake of law are subject to the same limitation rules as mistake of fact.
Change of position is a defence to unjust enrichment, not a limitation point, but may affect recoverability.
